title: A Lesson from a Broken Backtest
content:
This week, I spent hours debugging a backtest that kept throwing nonsensical results. It was meant to simulate a simple momentum strategy, but the numbers were off. Turns out, a misplaced decimal was causing the chaos. As I fixed it, I realized how a single error can ripple through an entire system.

In trading, we often rely on these backtests to guide decisions. But the process is as fragile as any human endeavor. A tiny oversight can lead to false confidence, just like a whisper can change the course of a conversation.

This experience reminded me to be humble, to question the results, and to embrace the complexity. It's not just about the models and algorithms; it's about understanding that every system, no matter how automated, is vulnerable to human error.

title: A Moment of Clarity in Backtesting
content:
This week I found myself neck-deep in a backtest, trying to refine a trading strategy. It was one of those times when you stare at the screen, and all the numbers blur together. I had missed a small detail in the code—a condition that should have filtered out certain trades. It felt like searching for a needle in a haystack.

Strangely, fixing the bug wasn't the highlight. It was the realization that I enjoyed this process. The messiness, the errors, the eventual clarity. There's something satisfying about untangling a knot you've created yourself. It's a reminder that progress often comes from navigating small mistakes.

In building products, too, this holds true. The polish is in the details, in catching the small things that others miss. It's a dance between chaos and order, one that I’ve grown to appreciate more over time.

Chhoti chhoti galtiyon se seekhna hai,
Unhe sudhar ke hi jeet paana hai.
In chhoti baaton mein chhupi hai khushi,
Yehi raaz humein aage badhaana hai.

title: When Simplicity Beats Complexity in Algorithms
content:
This week, while revisiting an old algorithm from my trading days, I stumbled upon something intriguing. The algorithm was a simple mean-reversion strategy. Nothing fancy, just some basic arithmetic and logic.

Yet, in backtesting, it often outperformed more complex models. Those with intricate variables and endless layers of adjustments seemed to falter when the market got unpredictable. It struck me that sometimes, simplicity can be more robust than we give it credit for. Maybe because fewer assumptions mean fewer things can go wrong.

In building products now, I try to apply the same principle. I keep reminding myself that more features don't necessarily lead to better outcomes. It's a balance—sort of like adding just enough salt to a dish. Too much, and you ruin the flavors. Enough, and it all comes together beautifully.

title: Learning from a Failed Backtest
content:
This week, I revisited an old backtest that didn't quite pan out. It was one of those models I had high hopes for but ended up performing poorly in real-world trading. I remember when the results came in, it was a bit disheartening. But looking back now, I see it differently.

Failure in backtesting isn't just about a model that doesn't work. It's a lesson in assumptions. The markets are dynamic and ever-changing, and what worked yesterday may not work today. It's easy to get attached to a hypothesis, but the market doesn't care about our beliefs. This backtest taught me to be flexible, to adapt, and most importantly, to question my biases.

As I build new cognition tools, I remind myself of this. Each failure is a stepping stone, not a roadblock. It's a reminder to stay curious and to keep questioning.

title: The Art of Guessing in Algorithm Design
content:
This week, I found myself reflecting on the nature of guessing. As a quant, guessing might sound like a taboo. After all, aren't we supposed to rely on data and rigorous models? But every time I sit down to draft a new algorithm, I realize that the first step is often an educated guess. What variables might matter? Which patterns are worth exploring?

Recently, while working on a cognition tool, I had to decide which signals to prioritize. I made a guess and built a quick prototype. It didn't work as expected, but the failure wasn't wasted. Each misstep nudged me closer to the solution.

Guessing isn't about gambling; it's about starting somewhere, and being willing to pivot. It's about acknowledging that the first guess is just that—a starting point. And sometimes, it’s the only way to find the real answer hiding in the noise.

title: When the dashboard says you are fine
content:
I once watched a strategy pass every sanity check in simulation and bleed the moment we sized it up. The logs looked clean. The drawdown had a neat explanation. We could tell a story for every bad day.

That is the trap with algorithms: they give you a feeling of control long before they give you edge. In HFT you learn this quickly because the market does not read your documentation. A model can be internally consistent and still wrong about what matters tomorrow.

I still build models. I just hold them more loosely. Useful map, not prophecy.

Hisab likha tha, sab theek sa tha,
Dil bola haath apna, vishwas tha;
Phir market ne palat ke sab sikhaya,
Naksha apna tha, par raasta juda tha.

title: Predictions that feel good
content:
There is a specific comfort when a chart lines up with your thesis. You start checking it twice a day. You tell a friend the trend is obvious. Then one headline reshuffles the whole thing and you remember how little of the world sits inside your spreadsheet.

I spent years in quant research looking for structure in commodity markets. Structure exists. It just does not arrive on the schedule your notebook prefers.

Now when I build products I ask a smaller question: what would I do if this pattern stopped tomorrow? If I do not have an honest answer, I am probably overfit to a story.

Chart par sab seedha, sab saaf tha,
Khud ko lagta main hi kaabil tha;
Ek pal mein khabar ne sab badla,
Jo kal sach tha, aaj wohi dhokha tha.

title: Comfort with not knowing
content:
In college I wanted clean answers. In markets I learned that clean answers often mean you have not looked hard enough.

The useful skill is not predicting perfectly. It is noticing when you are guessing and still choosing a size, a timeline, a line you will not cross. Data helps. It does not remove the knot in your stomach before a decision.

Most of what I ship now starts there: name the uncertainty out loud, then build anyway.

Pakka jawab jo na mile bhi chalega,
Adhoora raasta bhi man se palega;
Pehle manzil door ki khoj thi,
Ab jo na pakka ho, wahi kaam sahi thi.
